name: Advanced Code Review
description: Senior-level PR review scenarios — architecture, performance, and real-world tradeoffs

questions.question: What race condition exists in this custom hook?
options: The useState initializer creates a new empty array reference on every component render cycle
The fetch call is missing required Content-Type and Accept headers causing silent CORS (Cross-Origin Resource Sharing) failures
Rapid query changes cause out-of-order responses where stale results can overwrite fresh ones
The dependency array should include setResults to prevent stale closure reference access bugs
questions.explanation: Without an AbortController or a request ID check, rapid query changes can cause responses to arrive out of order. The last-fired request may not be the last to resolve, so stale results overwrite fresh ones. The fix is to use an AbortController in the useEffect cleanup, or track a request sequence number.
optionExplanations: useState only uses the initial value once on mount — it doesn't create a new array reference on each render, so this isn't a performance issue.
Fetch requests don't require Content-Type for GET requests, and CORS is a server configuration issue, not related to missing request headers here.
setState functions from useState are guaranteed stable across renders — React docs confirm they don't need to be in dependency arrays.

questions.question: What stability issue would you flag in this custom hook's return value?
options: onChange and validate are recreated every render, breaking memoization of consuming components
The initialValue parameter should be stored in a useRef to prevent reinitialization on updates
Using two separate useState calls causes excessive batched re-renders during validation flow
The setError(null) call inside onChange creates a race condition with the validate function
questions.explanation: onChange and validate are new function references on every render. Any component using these in a useEffect dependency array or passing them to React.memo children will re-render unnecessarily. Wrapping them in useCallback fixes referential stability. This is a critical review catch for shared hooks.
optionExplanations: useState correctly ignores initialValue after the first render — useRef isn't needed here since the initialization behavior is already correct.
React batches multiple setState calls within the same event handler into a single re-render, so two useState calls don't cause extra renders.
setError(null) in onChange and setError(err) in validate run in separate user interactions — there's no race condition between them.

questions.question: What is the most critical issue in this API (Application Programming Interface) layer design?
options: Spreading options.headers can silently override the required Authorization header from callers
Using template literals for URL construction opens the endpoint to path traversal injections
The hardcoded Content-Type header breaks multipart form-data and file upload request types
The thrown Error only includes the status code, losing the response body needed for recovery
questions.explanation: Throwing a generic Error with just the status code discards the response body and makes it impossible for callers to implement proper error recovery. A 401 should trigger token refresh, a 422 should show validation errors, a 429 should trigger retry-after. Senior reviewers flag this because poor error discrimination cascades into bad UX throughout the entire app.
optionExplanations: While spreading could override Authorization, callers would need to explicitly set it — the more impactful issue is losing error response details for recovery.
Path traversal is a server-side validation concern — the frontend endpoint parameter doesn't create a security vulnerability in the API client itself.
While the hardcoded Content-Type is limiting, it's typically fine for JSON APIs — the bigger problem is the error handling that prevents proper recovery.

questions.question: What architectural concern would you raise in your review?
options: Multiple boolean flags create impossible states — a single status enum or state machine is safer
The progress state should use useRef instead of useState to reduce unnecessary render cycles
The handleUpload function should be wrapped in useCallback to maintain a stable prop reference
Using separate useState calls for each flag adds overhead — useReducer would perform better
questions.explanation: Four independent booleans create 2^4 = 16 possible states, most of which are invalid (e.g., uploading AND success AND error simultaneously). A single status enum or state machine like XState makes impossible states unrepresentable. This is one of the most common senior-level review catches — boolean flag soup always leads to subtle bugs as requirements grow.
optionExplanations: Progress needs to trigger UI updates to show the upload bar — useRef doesn't cause re-renders, so the progress indicator would never update.
useCallback is a performance optimization, not an architectural fix — the fundamental problem is the impossible state combinations from multiple booleans.
useReducer doesn't inherently perform better than multiple useState calls — React batches both equally. The issue is state modeling, not performance.

questions.question: What bundle size concern would you flag in this PR?
options: Named exports add bundler overhead compared to default exports during module resolution steps
The path alias import adds an extra resolution step that slows down production build times
Barrel exports can defeat tree-shaking, pulling heavy unused components into the final bundle
Each re-exported component should use dynamic imports to enable automatic code splitting
questions.explanation: Barrel files (index.js re-exports) can break tree-shaking in Webpack 4 and some configurations of Webpack 5. Importing Button may cause the bundler to include the entire module graph of all exported components, including heavy ones like RichTextEditor and ChartWidget. The fix is direct imports (import { Button } from '@/components/Button') or configuring sideEffects: false in package.json.
optionExplanations: Named and default exports have identical bundler overhead — the export style doesn't affect tree-shaking or module resolution performance.
Path aliases are resolved at build time with negligible cost — they don't measurably impact production build times or bundle size.
Dynamic imports add unnecessary async loading complexity for frequently-used components — direct imports solve the tree-shaking issue more simply.

questions.question: What performance anti-pattern would you flag in this PR?
options: The useQuery hooks should be replaced with useSWR for better built-in cache invalidation
N+1 request waterfall — each member card fires its own stats request after members resolve
The team and members queries should be merged into a single combined GraphQL operation
MemberCard should be wrapped in React.memo to prevent re-renders when other stats update
questions.explanation: This is the frontend equivalent of an N+1 database query. The stats requests can't start until members load (waterfall), and then each member triggers its own HTTP request. With 50 members, that's 52 sequential network calls. The fix is a batch endpoint (fetchAllMemberStats(teamId)) or a GraphQL query that fetches everything in one request.
optionExplanations: Switching from React Query to SWR (Stale-While-Revalidate) doesn't fix the N+1 problem — both libraries would fire the same per-member requests with this component structure.
GraphQL is one possible solution, but the core issue is the N+1 pattern — a batch REST (Representational State Transfer) endpoint would fix it without requiring a GraphQL migration.
React.memo prevents re-renders but doesn't reduce the number of network requests — each card still fires its own stats fetch on mount.

questions.question: What migration concern would you raise in review?
options: The function should accept a version parameter to support dynamic API (Application Programming Interface) version switching
The locale field mapping is lossy because 'en-US' and 'en' are not semantically equivalent
The response shape should be validated at runtime with a schema library before being used
There is no v1 fallback, so environments that haven't deployed v2 yet will break entirely
questions.explanation: API migrations need backwards compatibility during the transition. If the backend rolls out v2 gradually, or if the frontend deploys before the backend, users will get errors. A safe migration pattern: try v2 first, fall back to v1, log which version was used. Once v2 adoption hits 100%, remove the v1 fallback in a follow-up PR.
optionExplanations: A version parameter adds flexibility but doesn't solve the core problem — environments without v2 deployed will still break with no fallback.
While 'en-US' vs 'en' differ in specificity, the locale mapping is intentional in the migration — the critical risk is environments breaking without v1 fallback.
Runtime validation is good practice but secondary — if v2 isn't deployed yet, validation won't help because the endpoint itself will return an error.

questions.question: What would you flag about this error boundary implementation?
options: A single top-level boundary means any route crash kills the entire app with no recovery path
ErrorBoundary must be a class component because function components cannot catch render errors
The fallback should use a Suspense boundary instead to provide consistent loading and error UX
Error boundaries interfere with React Router's outlet rendering and cause hydration mismatches
questions.explanation: One error boundary for the whole app means a crash in the Reports page also takes down Dashboard and Settings. Users lose all functionality. Granular error boundaries per route or feature let the rest of the app keep working. The fallback should also include a retry mechanism (remount the route) rather than a dead-end error message.
optionExplanations: While error boundaries must technically be class components, this is an implementation detail — the real issue is the single boundary's blast radius.
Suspense handles loading states for lazy components, not render errors — it cannot replace error boundaries for catching runtime exceptions.
Error boundaries work correctly with React Router and don't cause hydration mismatches — this is not a real compatibility issue.

questions.question: What would you flag in this shared component's API (Application Programming Interface)?
options: The component should use generic types to let consumers extend the props interface flexibly
Using ReactNode for the actions prop is too permissive and needs a stricter type contract
A 17-prop API surface is too large — it should be split into composable, focused variants
The component needs forwardRef support to allow imperative handle access from parent code
questions.explanation: A component with 17 props is a code smell in a shared library. It signals the component is trying to handle too many use cases, making it hard to maintain, document, and use correctly. Better patterns: compound components (Card.Image, Card.Actions), variant-specific wrappers (LinkCard, SelectableCard), or composition slots. The prop count directly correlates with bug surface area in a shared library.
optionExplanations: Generic types add flexibility but don't address the core problem — a 17-prop interface is too complex regardless of how extensible the types are.
ReactNode is the standard type for renderable content in React — it's appropriately permissive for a slot-based actions prop.
forwardRef is useful for DOM (Document Object Model) access but is a minor enhancement — the overwhelming API surface of 17 props is the far more pressing design concern.

questions.question: What security vulnerability would you flag in this PR?
options: Using dangerouslySetInnerHTML with user-generated comment content enables stored XSS (Cross-Site Scripting) attacks
The API (Application Programming Interface) endpoint is susceptible to SQL injection through the unvalidated postId path parameter
The fetch call should use POST instead of GET to mitigate cross-site request forgery attacks
The author display field needs HTML entity encoding to prevent UI-based clickjacking exploits
questions.explanation: dangerouslySetInnerHTML with user-generated content (c.body) is a textbook stored XSS vulnerability. An attacker posts a comment containing <script>stealCookies()</script>, which then executes for every user who views that post. The fix is to use a sanitization library (like DOMPurify) before rendering, or better yet, render markdown instead of raw HTML.
optionExplanations: SQL injection is a server-side concern handled by parameterized queries — the frontend fetch URL doesn't directly create SQL injection vulnerabilities.
GET requests for reading comments are semantically correct — CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) protection is handled by tokens or SameSite cookies, not HTTP method changes.
Clickjacking uses iframe overlays, not HTML in text fields — the author field displaying plain text doesn't create a clickjacking vulnerability.

questions.scenario:
```yaml
# .github/workflows/deploy.yml PR diff:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3
      - run: npm ci
      - run: npm run build
-     - run: npm test
      - run: npx serverless deploy --stage production
+     - run: npm test
```
questions.question: What CI/CD (Continuous Integration / Continuous Deployment) issue would you flag in this config change?
options: The checkout action should be pinned to a specific commit SHA rather than a mutable version tag
The npm ci step should include a frozen lockfile flag to guarantee fully reproducible installs
The serverless deploy command should use a matrix strategy for parallel multi-region rollouts
Tests now run after the production deploy, so broken code ships before failures are caught
questions.explanation: Moving the test step after deploy means broken code ships to production before tests catch it. This is a critical review catch — CI pipeline ordering defines your safety guarantees. Tests must always be a gate that blocks deployment, not a post-deploy verification. This subtle reordering might look like a simple cleanup but fundamentally breaks the deployment safety model.
optionExplanations: Pinning actions to a SHA is a supply-chain security best practice, but it's far less critical than the test step running after production deployment.
npm ci already uses the lockfile by default — the real danger in this diff is that broken code deploys to production before tests catch failures.
Matrix strategies for multi-region deployment are an enhancement, not a bug — the critical issue is the test/deploy ordering that removes the safety gate.

questions.scenario:
```sql
-- Migration PR: Add user preferences table
ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN preferences JSONB DEFAULT '{}';

-- New query used by the frontend:
SELECT u.*, p.team_name
FROM users u
JOIN projects p ON p.owner_id = u.id
WHERE u.preferences->>'theme' = 'dark';
```
questions.question: What would you flag reviewing this schema change from a frontend perspective?
options: JSONB columns require specialized ORM (Object-Relational Mapping) configuration that commonly causes type mapping errors
The JOIN should be a LEFT JOIN to correctly handle users who have no associated projects yet
Filtering on a JSONB field without a GIN index causes a full table scan that degrades over time
The DEFAULT '{}' should be DEFAULT NULL to reduce storage overhead and simplify null handling
questions.explanation: Filtering on preferences->>'theme' without a GIN or expression index means a full sequential scan of the users table. With 10K users this is fine; with 1M users, this query will time out and your frontend will show spinners forever. As a frontend reviewer, you should flag: 'Does this query have an index? What's the expected table size?' A missing index on a frequently-queried field is a production incident waiting to happen.
optionExplanations: Most modern ORMs handle JSONB columns natively without special configuration — this isn't a realistic concern for mainstream frameworks.
The JOIN type depends on business requirements — if every user should have projects, an INNER JOIN is correct and a LEFT JOIN would hide data issues.
DEFAULT '{}' is preferred over NULL for JSONB because it avoids null-check boilerplate and lets queries safely access nested keys without coalescing.

questions.question: What memoization anti-pattern would you flag?
options: The useMemo calls should be replaced with useCallback since they wrap function return values
Two of the three useMemos are wasteful — only the sort computation actually benefits from it
All three useMemos are necessary because React re-renders fully whenever parent props change
The products dependency requires a deep comparison hook to prevent false memoization misses
questions.explanation: formatPrice wraps a pure function with no dependencies — it will never change and the useMemo just adds overhead. containerStyle memoizes a static object — move it outside the component as a constant. Only sortedProducts benefits from memoization since sorting is O(n log n). Over-memoization is a common code smell: it adds cognitive complexity, memory overhead, and signals the author doesn't understand when memoization actually helps.
optionExplanations: useCallback is for memoizing functions passed as props — formatPrice and containerStyle aren't callbacks that need stable references for child components.
Static objects and pure functions without dependencies never change between renders — memoizing them adds overhead without any caching benefit.
React Query or parent components should ensure referential stability of products — adding deep comparison in the child just masks an upstream issue.

questions.question: What feature flag implementation issue would you flag?
options: localStorage flags can't be toggled remotely for incidents and users can bypass via DevTools
The analytics tracking call should be co-located in PricingPage for proper event attribution
The string comparison should use a parsed boolean value to avoid type coercion edge cases
Both layout components should be lazy-loaded with React.lazy to reduce initial bundle size
questions.explanation: Client-side localStorage flags have serious problems: (1) Can't kill a broken feature remotely during an incident — you have to deploy a code fix. (2) Users can toggle any flag via DevTools, bypassing rollout controls. (3) Flags persist across sessions, making gradual rollouts unreliable. (4) No way to target specific user segments. Use a feature flag service (LaunchDarkly, Unleash, etc.) with server-side evaluation and real-time kill switches.
optionExplanations: Analytics tracking location is a minor organizational concern — the critical issue is that localStorage flags can't be remotely controlled during incidents.
The string comparison works correctly as written — 'true' === 'true' is reliable and not prone to coercion issues in this context.
Lazy-loading is a bundle size optimization, not a feature flag concern — both layouts are small enough that code splitting provides minimal benefit here.

questions.question: What internationalization (i18n) issues would you flag in this component?
options: The component should accept an explicit locale prop to dynamically support multiple languages
Using className strings without CSS logical properties breaks layout in RTL (React Testing Library) language contexts
Hardcoded English strings and raw currency/number formatting violate i18n standards throughout
The Product type should define locale-specific fields for translated name and description values
questions.explanation: This component has at least 4 i18n issues: (1) 'Add to cart', 'Out of stock', and 'left' are hardcoded English — use t('addToCart') translation keys. (2) Currency formatting (product.currency + price) breaks for currencies with different symbol positions (¥100 vs $100 vs 100€). Use Intl.NumberFormat. (3) Number formatting (product.stock) should be locale-aware (1,000 vs 1.000). (4) The layout may break in RTL languages. Senior reviewers catch these because fixing i18n retroactively is extremely expensive.
optionExplanations: An explicit locale prop pushes i18n responsibility to every parent component — i18n libraries use context to provide locale globally without prop threading.
CSS logical properties help with RTL layouts but are a secondary concern — the hardcoded strings and raw formatting are the primary i18n violations.
Translation is typically handled by i18n libraries at render time using keys, not by duplicating fields in the data model for each supported language.

questions.question: What memory issue would you flag in this long-running SPA (Single-Page Application) component?
options: The WebSocket connection should be managed by a shared context provider to prevent duplicate sockets
Messages accumulate indefinitely in state without any upper bound, causing unbounded memory growth
The spread operator in setMessages creates expensive deep copies of the entire message history
Each message image tag creates a persistent network connection that is never released on scroll
questions.explanation: In a long-running chat, messages keep accumulating via [...prev, newMsg] with no upper limit. After hours of use, the array grows unboundedly, consuming memory and slowing renders. Implement a sliding window (keep last N messages), virtualized rendering, or paginated message history to cap memory usage.
optionExplanations: Context-managed WebSockets are an architectural improvement but don't address the unbounded memory growth from accumulating messages.
Spread creates a shallow copy, not a deep copy — while it's O(n), the real issue is that n grows without limit, not the per-operation cost.
Image tags load resources but don't maintain persistent connections — the browser manages image caching and connection pooling automatically.

questions.question: What architectural issue would you flag in this Next.js App Router page?
options: The two fetch calls should be combined into a single endpoint to reduce the total request count
The loading state should use a Suspense boundary with a skeleton component for better perceived speed
This page uses 'use client' unnecessarily — it should be a Server Component fetching data directly
The useEffect fetch calls need AbortController cleanup to prevent race conditions on navigation
questions.explanation: This page has no client-side interactivity — it just fetches data and renders it. Making it a Client Component forces the browser to download the JavaScript, hydrate, then fetch data in a waterfall. As a Server Component, the data fetches happen on the server with zero client-side JavaScript, eliminating the loading spinner and reducing time to first meaningful paint.
optionExplanations: Combining endpoints is a minor optimization — the fundamental issue is that server-side data fetching eliminates the client-side waterfall entirely.
Suspense improves perceived loading but doesn't fix the unnecessary client-side data fetching that a Server Component would eliminate completely.
AbortController is good cleanup practice, but the core problem is the unnecessary 'use client' directive forcing client-side rendering and data fetching.

questions.question: What bundle size issue would you flag in this utilities file?
options: The generic type constraint on sortByName adds runtime overhead that increases the compiled output
Full imports of moment and lodash pull entire libraries into the bundle for trivial operations
The helper functions should be inlined at call sites instead of centralized in a utilities file
Using named exports instead of default exports prevents bundlers from properly tree-shaking code
questions.explanation: moment (~300KB) and lodash (~70KB) are imported in their entirety for operations that native JavaScript handles easily. Use Intl.DateTimeFormat or date-fns for dates, and native string/array methods for capitalize and sort. If lodash is needed, use lodash-es with named imports (import sortBy from 'lodash-es/sortBy') for tree-shaking.
optionExplanations: TypeScript generics are erased at compile time and produce zero runtime overhead — they don't affect bundle size at all.
Centralizing helpers in a utilities file is good for maintainability — the problem is the heavyweight library imports, not the file organization.
Named exports are actually better for tree-shaking than default exports — most bundlers handle named exports more effectively.

questions.question: What database performance issue would you flag in this API (Application Programming Interface) route?
options: The sequential await calls for team and members should use Promise.all for parallel execution
The for loop issues N queries per member creating an N+1 problem that degrades with team size
The findById and findByTeamId calls should be replaced with a single JOIN query for efficiency
The Response.json serialization should stream results incrementally for large team summaries
questions.explanation: For a team of 20 members, this fires 1 (team) + 1 (members) + 20 (tasks) + 20 (reviews) = 42 database queries. At 50 members, it's 102 queries. This N+1 pattern causes response times to grow linearly with team size. Fix with batch queries: db.tasks.findByUserIds(memberIds) and db.reviews.findByUserIds(memberIds), reducing it to 4 total queries.
optionExplanations: Parallelizing team and members saves one round-trip but doesn't address the per-member loop that generates the vast majority of queries.
A single JOIN could work but adds complexity — the immediate fix is batch queries in the loop, which reduces queries from O(n) to O(1).
Streaming is useful for very large responses but doesn't reduce the database load — the query count is the primary performance bottleneck.

questions.question: What misuse of concurrent React features would you flag?
options: useTransition is meant for wrapping state updates that trigger re-renders, not async fetch calls
The startTransition call should be moved outside the useEffect and into an event handler instead
The isPending flag from useTransition conflicts with the loading state from the async fetch call
The useTransition hook should be replaced with useDeferredValue applied to the query parameter
questions.explanation: useTransition marks synchronous state updates as non-urgent so React can keep the UI responsive. Wrapping a fetch call inside startTransition doesn't make the fetch non-blocking — the transition completes immediately and isPending becomes false before results arrive. Use useTransition around the state update itself (e.g., filtering a large local list), or use Suspense-compatible data fetching for async operations.
optionExplanations: The location of startTransition isn't the issue — useEffect or event handler, the problem is that fetch is async and transitions are for synchronous updates.
While there's a conceptual overlap, the actual bug is that isPending doesn't track the fetch at all — it resolves immediately because fetch is not a synchronous update.
useDeferredValue defers a value to reduce re-renders, but it doesn't fix the fundamental misuse of transitions for async data fetching.

questions.question: What memory leak would you catch in this cleanup logic?
options: The chart.destroy() call should be wrapped in a try-catch to handle cleanup errors gracefully
The event listeners on window and document are never removed, leaking handlers across navigations
The chartRef should use a callback ref pattern instead of useRef for proper lifecycle management
The fetchInitialData call inside the constructor creates an unmanaged Promise that may reject
questions.explanation: While chart.destroy() is called, the resize and visibilitychange event listeners attached to window and document are never removed. These global listeners persist after unmount, holding references to the destroyed chart instance. Add removeEventListener calls for both listeners in the cleanup function.
optionExplanations: Error handling in cleanup is nice but doesn't address the leaked event listeners that persist after the component unmounts.
Callback refs help with dynamic ref targets, but the standard useRef works fine here — the leak is from the un-removed event listeners.
While the unmanaged Promise is a minor concern, the event listeners actively leak memory by keeping references to the chart after unmount.

questions.question: What performance issue would you flag in this route configuration?
options: Static imports load all page bundles upfront, making every user download code for routes they never visit
The routes array should be defined as a constant outside the module scope to prevent re-creation on import
Each route element should be wrapped in an ErrorBoundary to isolate page-level crashes from the router
The route paths should use a centralized constants file to prevent typos and enable compile-time checks
questions.explanation: Static imports mean all 8 page components are bundled into the main JavaScript file. Users loading /dashboard must download code for AdminPanel, AuditLog, and every other page. Use React.lazy() with dynamic imports: const Dashboard = lazy(() => import('./pages/Dashboard')). This creates separate chunks loaded on demand, dramatically reducing initial bundle size.
optionExplanations: The routes array is created once at module evaluation time — there's no re-creation issue. The problem is the static imports pulling all pages into one bundle.
Error boundaries per route are a good practice but don't address the bundle size problem caused by static imports of all page components.
Centralized route constants prevent typos but don't reduce bundle size — the static imports are what force all page code into the initial download.
